reviewerResults.persona: Small startup CTO with low to medium AWS experience. Cares most about time-to-production and shipping quickly without getting trapped in AWS complexity.
strengths: Strong 'When to use' / 'When not to use' section with a concise comparison table that helps a startup CTO quickly decide if EFS is the right fit vs. S3, Redis, or local disk.

Implementation claims in 'How it works' are fully verified against source code: encryption at rest, generalPurpose performance mode, 2 mount targets, NFS 2049 security group rules — all confirmed in src/domain/calculated-stack-overview-manager/resource-resolvers/efs-filesystems/utils.ts.

Both consumer-side use cases (container mounting and Lambda mounting) are covered with realistic, copyable examples. The subdirectory mount section is a practical addition that addresses real multi-tenant or isolation scenarios.

The Lambda VPC warning is critical, well-placed, and accurately reflects the codebase's own recommendation (types/stacktape-config/functions.d.ts lines 104-108 explicitly recommend web-service or worker-service as alternatives).

Throughput mode comparison table with mode/behavior/best-for/cost columns helps the reader pick the right option without deep AWS knowledge. The Tip about bursting + small filesystems is a valuable gotcha.
All CodeBlock examples use correct intellisense + tabs pattern with well-placed focus markers. Application-level prose is kept out of CodeBlock. No forbidden patterns detected.
Heading structure is compact and scannable — all H2s are 1-3 words, H3 is 2 words. Page rhythm matches the example page well.
problems: Transit encryption claim in 'How it works' is overly broad. The sentence 'Data in transit between containers/functions and the filesystem is also encrypted' is only verified for ECS containers (TransitEncryption: 'ENABLED' in multi-container-workloads/utils.ts line 1158). Lambda's FileSystemConfigs API does not expose a transit encryption setting — the claim should be scoped to container workloads only.

The intro paragraph also states 'encrypts everything at rest and in transit' without scoping the transit encryption to containers. This propagates the same inaccuracy into the AI-extractable first paragraph.

Unsourced pricing: '$~0.023/GB' for S3 in the 'When to use it' section and '~$0.05/GB/month for storage' for backup costs are not present in any provided source file (prices.json has no EFS entries, type JSDoc only mentions ~$0.30/GB for standard access). The throughput table's 'Extra cost' column also includes pricing characterizations not grounded in source.

The 'Elastic throughput mode adds per-read/write charges. Provisioned throughput has a flat fee based on the MiB/s you reserve.' line in the pricing context isn't directly sourced — only the $0.30/GB standard access cost comes from the type JSDoc.
mandatoryFixes: Scope the transit encryption claim in 'How it works' to container workloads only. Change 'Data in transit between containers/functions and the filesystem is also encrypted' to something like 'Data in transit between containers and the filesystem is encrypted (ECS mounts use transit encryption).' Remove or caveat the Lambda transit encryption implication.
Narrow the intro paragraph's 'encrypts everything at rest and in transit' to 'encrypts data at rest' or scope transit encryption to containers, so the AI-extractable first paragraph is accurate.

Remove unsourced pricing numbers: drop '~$0.023/GB' S3 comparison in 'When to use it' (or replace with a qualitative statement like 'significantly cheaper per GB'). Remove '~$0.05/GB/month for storage' from the Backups section. Keep only the ~$0.30/GB/month figure that appears in the type JSDoc.

Trim the pricing context paragraph to only include what's sourced. The elastic/provisioned throughput cost characterizations can stay as qualitative descriptions (they're in the JSDoc) but remove any specific dollar figures not in source.
optionalImprovements: Consider adding a brief note that the mount path for Lambda must start with /mnt/ — the draft mentions this in passing but could make it more prominent since it's a common error that blocks deploys.

The throughput Tip about bursting and small filesystems is good but could include a concrete example: 'A 10 GB filesystem with bursting mode only sustains ~500 KiB/s baseline throughput' to make the tradeoff tangible.

In the Lambda VPC warning, consider mentioning that container-based resources (web-service, worker-service) naturally have internet access, which is why they're the recommended alternative — this helps the reader understand the 'why' behind the suggestion.

The 'Basic example' creates an EFS with `new EfsFilesystem({})` — adding a one-line comment like '// Default: elastic throughput, encryption enabled' would make the example more self-documenting without adding clutter.

The page has no mention of how to inspect or debug EFS usage (e.g., CloudWatch metrics for burst credit balance, throughput utilization). A brief pointer to monitoring would help a CTO operating this in production.

reviewerResults.persona: SMB engineering manager with medium to high AWS knowledge. Cares about long-term maintainability, team clarity, operational fit, technically correct Stacktape class-based config examples, and valid MDX component usage.
strengths: Strong page structure: early pricing, clear when-to-use guidance, copyable class-based TypeScript config examples, and short headings.
The throughput and backups sections are well aligned with the authoritative EfsFilesystemProps source.
The page makes useful tradeoffs between EFS, buckets, databases, Redis, and local scratch storage.
problems: Several technical statements are not grounded in the provided EFS sources: Lambda mounting support, `joinDefaultVpc: true`, `/mnt/` mount-path requirement, Lambda internet-access behavior, automatic VPC endpoints for S3/DynamoDB, mount target count, access point creation, automatic subdirectory creation, and security group rule behavior.

The first paragraph says multiple Lambda functions can read and write EFS, but the EFS source only says multiple containers and access from containers via `volumeMounts`. Lambda EFS may exist elsewhere, but it is not in the provided source set.

The page states EFS encrypts everything at rest and in transit. The provided source only explicitly grounds encryption in transit for EFS volume mounts; at-rest encryption is not stated in the provided EFS source.
The pricing line includes Elastic throughput read/write charges, provisioned throughput flat fees, backup pricing, and an S3 comparison price that are not present in the provided source files.

The mounting examples use `volumeMounts` property shapes (`type: 'efs'`, `efsFilesystemName`, `mountPath`, `rootDirectory`) that are not visible in the provided source files, so they cannot be verified from this source set.

The How it works section overstates implementation details from AWS internals without source support, especially fixed two mount targets, access points per mount, performance mode fixed to `generalPurpose`, and at-rest encryption.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or narrow all unsupported Lambda EFS claims unless the source set includes Lambda volume mount types and validators proving them. Do not document `joinDefaultVpc`, `/mnt/`, or internet-access side effects from the current provided files.

Remove unsupported pricing details. Keep only the grounded storage price: approximately `$0.30/GB/month` for standard access, plus cautious wording that additional fees apply for provisioned throughput if mentioned from the JSDoc.

Remove unsupported implementation claims in How it works, or replace the section with only sourced behavior: EFS is persistent shared file storage for containers, uses `volumeMounts`, encrypts data in transit for mounts, supports backupEnabled, and supports elastic/provisioned/bursting throughput.

Do not claim automatic security group rules, NFS port 2049, mount target count, EFS access point behavior, or automatic subdirectory creation unless those statements are grounded in provided runtime validators or types.
Remove unsupported at-rest encryption claims unless a source file explicitly documents at-rest encryption for EFS filesystems.
optionalImprovements: Use `label: 'TypeScript'` for Stacktape config tabs to match the stated CodeBlock rule, even though the examples otherwise use the correct class-based pattern.
Add a short note that the API reference is the source for exhaustive options, since the prose intentionally covers only `backupEnabled`, `throughputMode`, and `provisionedThroughputInMibps`.
Tone down S3 comparisons to decision guidance without unsourced S3 pricing or POSIX details, or cite source files that support those details.
If mounting is important to the page, include the source file that defines `ContainerEfsMount` and any Lambda equivalent, then re-review the examples against that schema.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer with little AWS or DevOps experience. Needs jargon kept under control and wants examples that are easy to follow.
strengths: Strong 'when to use / when not to use' section with a practical comparison table that helps readers make the right storage choice quickly.
Pricing context is grounded in the source JSDoc (~$0.30/GB/month) and placed prominently at the top.
Mounting examples cover both containers and Lambda functions — the two main consumption patterns — with realistic, copyable configs.
Throughput comparison table is clear and actionable, with concrete guidance on which mode to pick.
Important Lambda VPC caveat is called out prominently in a Warning block, preventing a common footgun.
All headings are short and scannable (1-3 words for most H2s). Page rhythm follows the example page well.
CodeBlock usage is correct throughout — all Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with focus markers hiding imports.
problems: The 'How it works' section states 'Performance mode is fixed to generalPurpose' and claims exactly 'Two mount targets — one in each of two availability zones.' Neither claim appears in the provided source types (EfsFilesystemProps). These are plausible AWS implementation details but are not grounded in the provided source files.

The S3 pricing comparison '~$0.023/GB' in the 'When to use it' section is not present in the provided source files. The EFS pricing (~$0.30/GB/month) is source-grounded via JSDoc, but the S3 figure is invented for comparison.

The Lambda VPC warning says 'If you need both EFS and internet access, use a container-based compute resource instead.' This is misleading — a Lambda function in a VPC with NAT Gateway (usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT) can also reach the internet. Container services in a VPC face the same networking constraint.
The Lambda VPC warning claims 'It can still reach S3 and DynamoDB (Stacktape auto-creates VPC endpoints)' — this claim about auto-created VPC endpoints is not grounded in the provided source files.

'POSIX user enforcement' in the subdirectory mounts section is jargon that the target audience (little DevOps experience) may not understand. The earlier mention of 'POSIX filesystem semantics' is better handled because it is followed by concrete examples (directory listing, random reads, file locks).
mandatoryFixes: Remove or qualify ungrounded claims in 'How it works': either verify 'generalPurpose performance mode', 'two mount targets', and 'access points per mount' from source, or soften the wording (e.g., 'Stacktape creates the underlying EFS filesystem, security groups, mount targets, and access points needed for each volume mount').

Remove the S3 pricing figure (~$0.023/GB) that is not present in the provided source files. The cost comparison can remain qualitative: 'EFS costs more per GB than S3 but provides POSIX filesystem semantics.'

Fix the Lambda VPC warning: instead of recommending containers as the only path for EFS + internet, mention that adding NAT Gateway (usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT + stackConfig.vpc.nat) is also an option for Lambda. The current wording will send users down an unnecessarily complex path.
optionalImprovements: Briefly explain 'POSIX user enforcement' in the subdirectory mounts section — e.g., 'Each mount gets its own EFS access point, which restricts file access to a specific user ID' — to keep the page accessible to the target audience.

The VPC endpoint claim in the Lambda warning, if verified, should specify which services get endpoints. If unverifiable, remove it and keep the warning simpler: 'joinDefaultVpc places the function inside the VPC, which restricts outbound network access.'

Consider adding a brief note about the 'arn' referenceable parameter (the only one per source: EfsFilesystemReferencableParam = 'arn') in prose before the <ReferenceableParams /> component, so readers know what to expect.

The 'Backups' section could note that backupEnabled defaults to false (or is omitted by default), since the source type shows it as optional with no documented default — making the reader aware they must opt in.

The throughput table's 'Extra cost' column for 'elastic' says 'Per-read/write charges' — consider adding a brief note that elastic is still the cheapest option for most workloads because it avoids paying for unused provisioned capacity.

issues.statement: Unlike `/tmp` (capped at 512 MB and cleared between cold starts), EFS data persists indefinitely and can be shared across multiple functions.
issues.reason: The draft says Lambda /tmp is "capped at 512 MB". The source type definition shows the `storage` property accepts 512–10,240 MB, meaning /tmp can be configured up to 10 GB. The default is 512 MB, but it is not a hard cap. The phrase "capped at 512 MB" is factually incorrect.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "Unlike `/tmp` (512 MB by default, up to 10 GB, and cleared between cold starts), EFS data persists indefinitely and can be shared across multiple functions."

evidence.quote: #### Size of the `/tmp` directory in MB (512 - 10,240). Ephemeral per invocation.

issues.statement: Your workload processes files that don't fit in `/tmp` (Lambda's 512 MB limit)
issues.reason: Same issue as above — Lambda /tmp is configurable from 512 MB to 10,240 MB. Calling it a "512 MB limit" is incorrect.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "Your workload processes files that don't fit in `/tmp` (512 MB by default, configurable up to 10 GB)"

evidence.quote: #### Size of the `/tmp` directory in MB (512 - 10,240). Ephemeral per invocation.

issues.statement: `joinDefaultVpc: true` is required for Lambda functions that mount EFS. This places the function inside the VPC, which means it loses direct internet access. It can still reach S3 and DynamoDB (Stacktape auto-creates VPC endpoints), but calls to external APIs (Stripe, OpenAI, etc.) will not work. If you need both EFS and internet access, use a container-based compute resource instead.
issues.reason: The warning's recommendation to 'use a container-based compute resource instead' omits that container services in VPC also lose internet access unless they use NAT Gateways (via `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT`). The source type JSDoc for `joinDefaultVpc` says 'If you need both VPC access and internet, use a `web-service` or `worker-service` instead' — which is consistent with the draft, but the draft doesn't explain *why* containers solve this (they can use NAT). A reader might think containers magically have internet in VPC. The NAT mechanism and its ~$32/month cost should be mentioned.
issues.suggestedFix: Amend the warning to explain the mechanism: 'If you need both EFS and internet access, use a container-based compute resource (web-service, worker-service) with `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT: true`, which routes outbound traffic through a NAT Gateway (~$32/month per AZ).'

evidence.quote:
**Tradeoff:** The function loses direct internet access. It can still reach S3 and DynamoDB
 * (Stacktape auto-creates VPC endpoints), but calls to external APIs (Stripe, OpenAI, etc.) will fail.
 * If you need both VPC access and internet, use a `web-service` or `worker-service` instead.

Deploy in private subnets with a static outbound IP via NAT Gateway.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * The container won't have a public IP. All outbound traffic routes through a NAT Gateway,
   * giving you a static IP you can whitelist in external services (APIs, payment gateways, etc.).
   *
   * Configure the number of NAT Gateways in `stackConfig.vpc.nat`.
   *
   * **Adds cost:** NAT Gateway ~$32/month + data processing fees.

issues.statement: Data in transit between containers/functions and the filesystem is also encrypted.
issues.reason: For containers (ECS), transit encryption is explicitly set to 'ENABLED' in the CloudFormation template. For Lambda functions, the transit encryption is NOT explicitly configured — Lambda's FileSystemConfig does not support a TransitEncryption parameter. The blanket claim covers both but the Lambda behavior depends on AWS defaults, not explicit Stacktape configuration.

issues.reason: The performance mode IS hardcoded to 'generalPurpose' in the source code (verified). The claim is accurate about the hardcoding but the 'optimal for' editorial adds unsourced characterization.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and mostly accurate. Two medium-severity factual errors exist: Lambda /tmp is described as 'capped at 512 MB' in two places, but the source shows it is configurable from 512 MB to 10,240 MB. Two pricing claims (S3 at ~$0.023/GB, backup at ~$0.05/GB/month) are not present in any provided source file and should be removed per the pricing rules. The VPC warning about Lambda internet access is directionally correct but should explain WHY container services solve the problem (NAT Gateways) rather than leaving the reader to guess. The infrastructure implementation claims (encryption, mount targets, security groups, access points) were verified against the actual domain source code and are accurate.

The draft has multiple unsupported or overstated claims, mostly around Lambda mounting, automatic networking resources, encryption details, default behavior, and pricing beyond the provided source. The core EFS resource shape, throughput modes, backup retention, and referenceable ARN are mostly aligned with the provided types.

reviewerResults.persona: Small startup CTO with low to medium AWS experience. Cares most about time-to-production and shipping quickly without getting trapped in AWS complexity.
strengths: Strong opening paragraph — defines what EFS is, why it matters, and the pay-per-use model in ~57 words. Works well as a standalone AI-extractable answer.
Decision table in 'When to use it' is concrete and actionable — lists four specific alternatives with links, not vague hand-waving.

Lambda VPC requirement called out in an Info box — this is a common gotcha that would trip up the target audience.
problems: The page does not mention that Stacktape enables encryption at rest by default (Encrypted: true is hardcoded in src/domain/.../efs-filesystems/utils.ts:23). For a security-conscious CTO, knowing both at-rest and in-transit encryption are automatic is valuable.

The basic example creates an EFS filesystem in isolation (`new EfsFilesystem({})`) which is technically valid but not useful on its own — the page even says so immediately after. Consider leading with the mounting example as the 'basic example' since no one creates an EFS without mounting it.

Missing any mention of performance characteristics or latency expectations. EFS has higher latency than local disk (single-digit milliseconds vs microseconds). A startup CTO choosing between EFS and local disk for hot data needs this context.

No mention of the `overrides` escape hatch — minor, but the type definition includes `overrides?: ResourceOverrides`. This is a cross-cutting concern and acceptable to omit, but a one-line mention linking to overrides docs would help advanced users.

The referenceable parameters section only exposes `arn` — the page doesn't mention this is the only available parameter. A brief note like 'EFS exposes only the `arn` parameter for cross-resource references' would set expectations.
mandatoryFixes: Add a sentence noting that encryption at rest is enabled by default (source: `Encrypted: true` hardcoded in efs-filesystems/utils.ts:23). This is a security-relevant fact the target audience cares about and is grounded in source.

Consider restructuring so the mounting example is the primary 'Basic example' — no user creates an EFS without mounting it. The current standalone EFS example can be shown as a brief precursor or omitted entirely.
optionalImprovements: Add a brief note about EFS latency characteristics (single-digit milliseconds for file operations) to help readers decide whether EFS is suitable for latency-sensitive workloads vs. using container-local storage for temp files.

Mention that `PerformanceMode` is hardcoded to `generalPurpose` (source: utils.ts:19) — users cannot change it, and knowing this prevents confusion if they look up AWS EFS docs and see a `maxIO` option.
Add a one-line note that the only referenceable parameter is `arn`, so readers know what's available for `$ResourceParam` without scrolling to the bottom.

Consider adding a brief cost comparison note: EFS at ~$0.30/GB/month can get expensive for large datasets (e.g., 100 GB = ~$30/month). For large static assets accessed via API, a bucket (~$0.023/GB/month) is 13x cheaper. This reinforces the 'when not to use' guidance with concrete numbers.

The Tip about bursting mode could mention the burst credit formula more explicitly — e.g., 'A 100 GB filesystem provides ~5 MiB/s baseline and can burst to 100 MiB/s until credits run out' — to make the tradeoff tangible.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer with little AWS or DevOps experience. Needs jargon kept under control and wants examples that are easy to follow.
strengths: 'When to use it' decision table is concrete and well-scoped — the alternatives (Bucket, DynamoDB, Redis, local disk) are the exact ones a newcomer would confuse with EFS.

Mounting section is the most important operational content for this resource and it's given proper weight — the example is realistic, the efsFilesystemName/mountPath relationship is explained clearly, and the Lambda VPC gotcha is surfaced in an Info callout.

Throughput comparison table distills three modes into a scannable format with clear 'best for' guidance and a sensible default recommendation (elastic). The Tip about bursting credit exhaustion is a practical warning most docs skip.
All CodeBlock examples correctly use focus markers to hide imports, follow the class-based defineConfig pattern, and avoid every forbidden config format.
Page density is well-matched to the resource's simplicity — three configurable properties don't get inflated into ten sections. The page is appropriately short.
problems: The page states EFS can be mounted into Lambda functions and mentions joinDefaultVpc in an Info callout, but provides no Lambda mounting example. For a developer new to AWS, the callout alone is insufficient — they'll need to see the volumeMounts shape inside a LambdaFunction config to actually use it.

The page does not mention EFS latency characteristics. EFS has noticeably higher latency (~single-digit ms) compared to local container disk. For the target audience, this is a critical tradeoff that could lead to poor architectural choices if omitted (e.g., using EFS for temp files or high-frequency reads where local disk or S3 would be better).

The 'Basic example' creates an EFS with empty props (`new EfsFilesystem({})`) and then immediately says 'To use it, mount it into a compute resource.' This makes the basic example feel incomplete on its own — the reader has to scroll to the next section to see anything useful. The example page's basic example is self-contained.

The pricing line says 'Additional fees apply when using provisioned throughput' but does not give even an approximate per-MiB/s cost. The throughput section repeats this vague statement. If the source has no number, that's fine, but the repeated vagueness weakens the pricing guidance.
mandatoryFixes: Add a concrete Lambda function mounting example (or combine it into the existing mounting section) showing `volumeMounts` with `joinDefaultVpc: true` on a LambdaFunction. The Info callout alone is not actionable for the target audience.

Merge the 'Basic example' and 'Mounting' sections so the first example the reader sees is a complete, working pattern — an EFS filesystem mounted into a compute resource. An isolated `new EfsFilesystem({})` with no mount is not a useful starting point. The current 'Basic example' can become a brief note ('You can also define the filesystem separately and mount it later') rather than the primary example.
optionalImprovements: Add a short sentence about EFS latency characteristics in the 'When to use it' section or near the top — something like 'EFS has higher latency than local container disk (~single-digit milliseconds per operation), so avoid it for high-frequency temporary file I/O.'

In the throughput section, consider noting the approximate cost of provisioned throughput if available from source data, or explicitly state that pricing varies by region and link to the AWS EFS pricing page.
Consider adding a brief note that EFS data is encrypted at rest by default (AWS behavior) — this is reassuring for the target audience and costs nothing to mention.

The 'Backups' section could mention how to restore from a backup, even if just to say 'Restore is done through the AWS Console or CLI — Stacktape does not currently manage EFS restores.' This anticipates the next question the reader will have after enabling backups.

issues.statement: Lambda functions use a similar volumeMounts property but require joinDefaultVpc: true because Lambda needs VPC access to reach the EFS filesystem.
issues.reason: The Info box omits an important caveat from the source: when joinDefaultVpc is true, the Lambda function loses direct internet access and cannot reach external APIs unless NAT Gateways are configured. This is a significant operational surprise that belongs next to the joinDefaultVpc mention.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a sentence: 'Setting joinDefaultVpc: true means the function loses direct internet access — it cannot call external APIs unless you also configure NAT Gateways in stackConfig.vpc.nat.'

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded in source material. All EFS configuration properties (throughputMode, provisionedThroughputInMibps, backupEnabled), their defaults, and their behavior descriptions match the efs-filesystem.d.ts source exactly. Pricing matches the source JSDoc. Volume mount property names were verified against the actual ContainerEfsMount and LambdaEfsMount types in the codebase. Lambda EFS support, the joinDefaultVpc requirement, and the /mnt/ path constraint were all confirmed in functions.d.ts. Two issues found: (1) the Lambda joinDefaultVpc Info box should mention the loss of internet access as a caveat (medium), and (2) the in-transit encryption claim is sourced only for containers, not explicitly for Lambda (low). No forbidden patterns (hand-authored YAML, StacktapeConfig style, fenced TS with defineConfig, marketing language) were detected.

The draft is mostly grounded in the provided EFS type source for core EFS behavior, pricing, throughput modes, backups, and container volume mounts. The main factual risks are Lambda-specific mounting claims, API reference naming, and a few operational/AWS behavior claims that are not supported by the provided files.

suggestions: Trim the opening paragraph from ~62 words to 40-55 words. Cut the use-case list ('Use it for shared uploads…') into a separate sentence or move it to 'When to use it'. The first paragraph should be a tight definition + value prop that an LLM can extract as a single snippet.

Add key AWS limits that are publicly documented: elastic throughput can deliver up to 10 GiB/s reads and 3 GiB/s writes; max file size is unlimited; max per-filesystem IOPS varies by mode. These concrete numbers make paragraphs more extractable by AI answer engines and differentiate the page from generic EFS descriptions.
Rename 'Referenceable parameters' to something search-friendly like 'Output parameters' or 'Resource outputs'. 'Referenceable parameters' is internal jargon that no user would search for.

Add a short FAQ section (3-5 questions) at the bottom targeting common search queries: 'Can Lambda functions use EFS?', 'What is the default EFS throughput mode?', 'How much does EFS cost per GB?', 'How do I share files between containers on AWS?'. Use concise Q&A pairs — these are high-value for People Also Ask and AI engine extraction.

In the 'Mounting' section, the first sentence is a long list of compatible resources. Lead instead with what mounting does ('Mounting an EFS filesystem makes its files available at a path inside your container or Lambda function'), then list compatible resources. This makes the paragraph extractable without needing to know what 'volumeMounts' means.

Add a brief note about EFS latency characteristics (single-digit millisecond latency for standard access) in the opening or throughput section. This is a frequent comparison point vs. EBS and S3, and including it helps the page rank for 'EFS vs S3' or 'EFS performance' queries.
